sections.title: Registration requirements for remittance networks
sections.summary: This section outlines additional requirements for applying to register as a remittance network provider, independent remittance dealer, or virtual remittance network provider. It specifies the application process and documentation needed for registration decisions under Division 3. The requirements apply to entities seeking to operate in the remittance sector.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes registration requirements for remittance networks, which may impact data transfer assessments when processing customer information across borders.

requirements.text: Additional requirements for application for registration as a remittance network provider
Additional requirements for application by remittance network
Additional requirements for application by independent remittance dealer
Additional requirements for application for registration as a virtual remittance network provider

sections.title: Customer due diligence for real estate transactions
sections.summary: This section mandates policies for customer due diligence in real estate transactions. It requires entities to implement specific measures to verify customer identity and assess risks associated with real estate-related financial activities. The obligations apply to financial institutions handling real estate transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires customer due diligence policies for real estate transactions, which may affect data transfer assessments when processing customer information across borders.

requirements.text: Policies relating to customer due diligence for real estate

sections.heading: Division 1—Initial customer due diligence
sections.title: Initial customer due diligence procedures
sections.summary: This section details the initial customer due diligence procedures for identifying and verifying customer identity. It specifies requirements for determining whether a customer is a body corporate, partnership, or unincorporated entity, and for identifying beneficial owners and senior managers. The obligations apply to financial institutions conducting customer due diligence.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes initial customer due diligence procedures, which may impact data transfer assessments when processing customer information across borders.

requirements.text: Customer is body corporate, partnership or unincorporated
Beneficial owners and senior manager, for bodies corporate
The nature and purpose of the business relationship or occasional

sections.title: Initial customer due diligence procedures
sections.summary: This section details the initial customer due diligence procedures for identifying and verifying customer identity. It specifies requirements for determining whether a customer is a body corporate, partnership, or unincorporated entity, and for identifying beneficial owners and senior managers. The obligations apply to financial institutions conducting customer due diligence.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes initial customer due diligence procedures, which may impact data transfer assessments when processing customer information across borders.

Beneficial owners and senior manager, for bodies corporate

sections.title: Delayed verification for remittance services
sections.summary: This section outlines procedures for delayed verification of customer identity in remittance services. It specifies conditions under which initial customer due diligence can be conducted after the commencement of services, particularly for foreign services. The requirements apply to remittance providers handling customer information.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses delayed verification procedures for remittance services, which may impact data transfer assessments when processing customer information across borders.

requirements.text: Delayed verification—various designated services provided in
Delayed initial customer due diligence—service provided in foreign

sections.title: Simplified customer due diligence for beneficial owners
sections.summary: This section specifies simplified initial customer due diligence procedures for identifying beneficial owners. It outlines requirements for entities to verify the identity of beneficial owners in a streamlined manner, applicable to certain high-risk transactions. The obligations apply to financial institutions conducting customer due diligence.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes simplified customer due diligence procedures for beneficial owners, which may impact data transfer assessments when processing customer information across borders.

requirements.text: Simplified initial customer due diligence for identity of beneficial

sections.title: Enhanced customer due diligence for virtual assets
sections.summary: This section mandates enhanced customer due diligence for certain virtual assets. It requires establishing the source of wealth and source of funds for these assets. The requirements apply to financial institutions conducting virtual asset transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes specific enhanced due diligence requirements for virtual asset transactions, which may impact data transfer assessments when handling customer financial information across borders.

requirements.text: Enhanced customer due diligence required when customer seeks virtual assets
Establish source of wealth and source of funds for virtual assets

sections.source_quote: 71 6-20 Enhanced customer due diligence required when customer seeks 6-21 Establishing source of wealth and source of funds when enhanced

sections.title: Initial customer due diligence for politically exposed persons
sections.summary: This section specifies initial customer due diligence requirements for politically exposed persons. It applies to financial institutions that must verify the identity and source of funds for these high-risk clients. The requirements are part of the anti-money laundering framework.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires specific due diligence for politically exposed persons, which may affect data transfer assessments when handling sensitive personal information across borders.

requirements.text: Matters for initial customer due diligence—politically exposed persons

sections.source_quote: 73 6-23 Matters for initial customer due diligence—politically exposed

sections.title: Initial customer due diligence for nested services
sections.summary: This section outlines initial customer due diligence requirements for nested services relationships. It applies to financial institutions that must verify the identity and source of funds for clients involved in nested service arrangements. The requirements are part of the anti-money laundering framework.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires specific due diligence for nested services, which may impact data transfer assessments when handling customer information across borders.

requirements.text: Matters for initial customer due diligence—nested services

sections.source_quote: 75 6-25 Matters for initial customer due diligence—nested services

sections.title: Ongoing customer due diligence for transferred customers
sections.summary: This section specifies ongoing customer due diligence requirements for transferred customers. It applies to financial institutions that must verify the identity and source of funds for customers who have been transferred between entities. The requirements are part of the anti-money laundering framework.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires specific ongoing due diligence for transferred customers, which may affect data transfer assessments when handling customer information across borders.

requirements.text: Ongoing customer due diligence—transferred pre-commencement

sections.source_quote: 77 6-27 6-28 Ongoing customer due diligence—transferred pre-commencement

sections.title: Agreement for collection and verification of KYC information
sections.summary: This section requires agreements or arrangements for the collection and verification of KYC information. It applies to financial institutions that must establish formal agreements with third parties to verify customer identity and financial information. The requirements are part of the anti-money laundering framework.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates agreements for KYC information collection and verification, which may impact data transfer assessments when sharing customer information across borders.

requirements.text: Requirements for agreement or arrangement on collection and verification of KYC information
Requirements for reliance on collection and verification of KYC information

sections.source_quote: 79 6-29 Requirements for agreement or arrangement on collection and verification of KYC 6-30 6-31 Requirements for reliance on collection and verification of KYC

sections.title: Initial customer due diligence for life policies and sinking funds
sections.summary: This section specifies initial customer due diligence requirements for life policies and sinking funds. It applies to financial institutions that must verify the identity and source of funds for clients with life insurance policies and sinking funds. The requirements are part of the anti-money laundering framework.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires specific due diligence for life policies and sinking funds, which may affect data transfer assessments when handling customer financial information across borders.

requirements.text: Initial customer due diligence—life policies and sinking fund

sections.source_quote: 85 6-34 Initial customer due diligence—life policies and sinking fund

sections.title: Initial customer due diligence for foreign services
sections.summary: This section requires institutions to conduct initial customer due diligence when providing services in foreign jurisdictions. It applies to correspondent banking arrangements where the service is delivered outside the home country. The obligations focus on verifying customer identity and assessing risks before initiating cross-border financial transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates initial due diligence for foreign services, requiring institutions to verify customer identity and assess risks before engaging in cross-border financial transactions. It impacts DTIAs by establishing baseline requirements for international correspondent banking activities.

requirements.text: Conduct initial customer due diligence for services provided in foreign jurisdictions

sections.source_quote: Initial customer due diligence—previous carrying out of applicable

sections.title: Obligations for collecting and verifying customer information
sections.summary: This section outlines obligations for ordering and beneficiary institutions to collect, verify, and monitor customer information when handling cross-border payments. It requires institutions to ensure accurate identification and ongoing monitoring of transactions to prevent money laundering activities. These obligations apply to financial institutions engaged in correspondent banking arrangements.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires institutions to collect, verify, and monitor customer information for cross-border payments, impacting DTIAs by establishing specific due diligence procedures for international transactions.

requirements.text: Collect, verify and monitor customer information for cross-border payments

sections.source_quote: Obligations of ordering institutions—collecting, verifying and

sections.title: Threshold transaction reporting requirements
sections.summary: This section mandates reporting of threshold transactions to authorities, including information about the customer and transaction details. It applies to financial institutions that handle transactions exceeding specified monetary thresholds. The reporting requirements are part of AML/CTF compliance and must be completed for cross-border transactions involving significant sums.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires reporting of threshold transactions, impacting DTIAs by mandating specific information collection and reporting for cross-border transactions exceeding monetary thresholds.

requirements.text: Report information about the customer for threshold transactions

sections.source_quote: Reports of threshold transactions—information about the customer

sections.title: Cross-border movement reporting requirements
sections.summary: This section establishes requirements for reporting cross-border movements of funds. It applies to financial institutions that facilitate international transfers and mandates specific information to be reported to relevant authorities. The reporting is part of AML/CTF compliance and ensures transparency in cross-border financial flows.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates cross-border movement reporting, impacting DTIAs by requiring specific information to be reported for international fund transfers.

requirements.text: Report cross-border movement of funds

sections.source_quote: Division 5—Cross-border movement reports

sections.title: Notices for cross-border movement reporting
sections.summary: This section requires institutions to affix notices about cross-border movement reporting to ensure transparency and compliance with reporting obligations. It applies to financial institutions that handle international transactions and mandates specific disclosures to inform customers about reporting requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires notices about cross-border movement reporting, impacting DTIAs by mandating specific disclosures for international transactions.

requirements.text: Affix notices about cross-border movement reporting

sections.source_quote: Affixing of notices about cross-border movement reporting

sections.title: Computer use for administrative actions
sections.summary: This section specifies that institutions may use computers to take administrative actions, including processing applications for reconsideration of decisions made by delegates. It applies to financial institutions that handle administrative tasks and mandates specific procedures for computer-based actions related to regulatory decisions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows computer use for administrative actions, impacting DTIAs by specifying procedures for handling regulatory decisions through digital means.

requirements.text: Use computers to take administrative actions

sections.source_quote: Use of computers to take administrative action—prescribed

sections.title: Transitional reporting forms for suspicious activity
sections.summary: This section specifies that reports of suspicious matters must continue to use the old form structure during a transitional period. Reporting entities must maintain the previous reporting format for suspicious activity reports while transitioning to new requirements. The section applies to entities that are required to submit such reports under the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is transitional and does not directly impact data transfer assessments as it pertains to reporting formats rather than data flows or cross-border processing.

requirements.text: Reports of suspicious matters must use old form structure during transitional period

sections.source_quote: Transitional—reports of suspicious matters to be in old form for

sections.heading: Section 1-1
sections.title: Definitions for AML/CTF regulations
sections.summary: This section provides detailed definitions critical for understanding the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006. It clarifies terms such as financial institution, reporting entity, senior manager, and transfer of value, including specific contexts for virtual assets and cross-border transactions. These definitions apply to entities subject to the Act and its associated regulations.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions establish foundational terms for data transfers involving financial institutions, including virtual assets and cross-border transactions, which are essential for DTIA compliance.

requirements.text: Designated service has specific meaning under the Act
Financial institution is defined as an entity subject to the Act
Reporting entity must comply with AML/CTF obligations
Senior manager refers to individuals with governance responsibilities
Transfer of value includes virtual asset transfers
Merchant payment requires specific conditions for card transactions
Tracing information must enable identification of transactions

sections.source_quote: This instrument is the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Rules 2025.

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Reporting group membership and obligations for financial entities
sections.summary: Reporting groups must have a lead entity for at least 28 days to comply with AML/CTF policies. Members must continue to follow the previous lead entity's policies during transitions. Non-reporting entity members must complete due diligence and training for obligations. This applies to financial entities operating across borders under Australia's AML/CTF framework.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires maintaining a lead entity for reporting groups during transitions to ensure continuity of AML/CTF compliance across international entities. Critical for cross-border financial operations where entities must adhere to the previous lead entity's policies.

requirements.text: Reporting group must not operate without a lead entity for more than 28 days
Members must continue to comply with previous lead entity's AML/CTF policies
Non-reporting entity members must complete due diligence for obligations
Non-reporting entity members must provide training meeting AML/CTF policy requirements

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of paragraph 236B(5)(c) of the Act, it is a condition for discharging an obligation imposed on a reporting entity that is a member of a reporting group that the reporting group has a lead entity.

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Enrolment and correction of financial reporting entities
sections.summary: Applications for financial reporting entities must include detailed information about business structure, designated services, earnings, and reporting group membership. AUSTRAC may correct or remove entries from registers if errors or cessation of services are identified. This ensures accurate reporting of financial entities across Australia and internationally.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires comprehensive registration of financial entities with detailed cross-border operational data. Critical for ensuring accurate reporting of international financial activities and compliance with AML/CTF regulations.

requirements.text: Application must include description of designated services and commencement dates
Application must specify if services are provided through Australian or foreign establishments
Application must detail earnings for the period, with specific definitions for different entity types
Application must include information about reporting group membership and lead entities
AUSTRAC may correct errors in reporting entities register
AUSTRAC may remove names from register if services cease

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of paragraph 51C(4)(a) of the Act, if the AUSTRAC CEO reasonably believes that there is an error in, or an omission from, an entry in the Reporting Entities Roll, the AUSTRAC CEO may correct the error or omission.

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Registration applications for remittance providers: information requirements
sections.summary: This section mandates specific information for applications to register as remittance network providers, independent remittance dealers, or remittance affiliates. It requires candidate details including names, addresses, business activities, financial identifiers, and information on foreign registrations. It also requires ML/TF risk assessments, AML/CTF policies, and details of accounts with financial institutions. The application must include a declaration of truthfulness and information on key personnel with past unlawful activity.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines detailed information requirements for remittance providers, including cross-border operational details and ML/TF risk assessments. It impacts DTIAs by requiring specific data on foreign operations and risk assessments that must be verified.

requirements.text: Application must contain candidate's full name
Application must contain candidate's ABN, ACN, ARBN and ARSN (as applicable)
Application must contain candidate's legal entity identifier (if applicable)
Application must contain information on foreign registrations if candidate operates internationally
Application must contain ML/TF risk assessment information
Application must contain AML/CTF policies for risk management
Application must contain information on accounts with financial institutions
Application must contain information on key personnel with past unlawful activity
Application must contain declaration that information is true and correct
Application must contain information on foreign countries where candidate operates
Application must contain details of training providers for compliance

sections.source_quote: Section 4-3(1) of the Act sets out information that must be contained in registration applications for remittance network providers, independent remittance dealers or remittance affiliates.

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Registration decisions for financial service providers
sections.summary: This section specifies matters that AUSTRAC must consider when deciding whether to register a person as a remittance network provider or virtual asset service provider. It requires assessment of criminal history, compliance with laws, business experience, operational readiness, and financial resources. The decisions apply to candidates applying for registration under sections 75B or 76D of the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the criteria for AUSTRAC's registration decisions, which impact data transfer assessments by requiring detailed information about financial operations, customer data handling, and compliance with anti-money laundering regulations.

requirements.text: Assess criminal history of candidate and key personnel
Evaluate compliance history with laws and regulations
Verify business experience appropriate for the candidate's operations
Confirm operational readiness to comply with the Act and AML/CTF Rules
Assess financial resources for providing registrable services

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of paragraphs 75C(2)(b) and 76E(2)(b) of the Act, in deciding whether to register a person (the candidate) in accordance with an application made under section 75B or 76D of the Act, the following matters are specified

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Suspension and cancellation of financial service registrations
sections.summary: This section outlines the conditions under which AUSTRAC may suspend or cancel a person's registration as a remittance network provider or virtual asset service provider. It specifies grounds for suspension including criminal convictions, regulatory breaches, and operational deficiencies. Cancellation requires assessment of ongoing compliance with anti-money laundering obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details suspension and cancellation procedures that affect data transfer assessments by requiring ongoing compliance with financial regulations and timely reporting of changes to registration details.

requirements.text: AUSTRAC may suspend registration if candidate has been charged or convicted of serious offences
Suspension requires written notice to candidate and relevant parties
Suspension period may be extended up to 3 months
AUSTRAC must remove suspension information from registers once revoked
Cancellation requires assessment of ongoing compliance with AML obligations

sections.source_quote: The AUSTRAC CEO may suspend a person's registration under Part 6 or 6A of the Act if the AUSTRAC CEO reasonably suspects that: (a) the person, any of its key personnel or any associate of the person or its key personnel, has been charged or convicted of an offence against the Act, or of an offence against a law of the Commonwealth, a State or Territory or a foreign country of any of the following kinds: (ii) terrorism or financing of terrorism; (vi) a serious offence of any other kind

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Personnel due diligence for AML/CTF
sections.summary: Reporting entities must assess personnel skills, knowledge and expertise relevant to AML/CTF responsibilities before and during employment. This applies to financial institutions and entities subject to anti-money laundering regulations.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires personnel due diligence for AML/CTF roles, impacting data transfer and processing practices for high-risk customers.

requirements.text: Reporting entity must assess personnel skills, knowledge and expertise relevant to AML/CTF responsibilities before employment
Reporting entity must assess personnel skills, knowledge and expertise relevant to AML/CTF responsibilities during employment

sections.source_quote: The AML/CTF policies of the reporting entity must require the reporting entity, as part of undertaking that due diligence, to assess, both before a person's employment or engagement and during a person's employment or engagement: (a) the person's skills, knowledge and expertise relevant to the particular responsibilities of the person under the AML/CTF policies;

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: AML/CTF personnel training and independent evaluations
sections.summary: Reporting entities must implement initial and ongoing AML/CTF training for employees, ensuring it is function-specific, risk-appropriate, and understandable. Policies must also require independent evaluations covering ML/TF risk assessments, policy design, compliance testing, risk mitigation, and reporting of findings to governing bodies. These obligations apply to all entities subject to the Act's AML/CTF requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates specific training and independent evaluations for AML/CTF compliance, directly impacting cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions by requiring robust internal controls and risk assessments.
sections.authority: Australian Transaction Reports and Security (ATRS) Act

requirements.text: Reporting entities must provide initial and ongoing AML/CTF training for employees
Training must be function-specific, risk-appropriate, and understandable
Reporting entities must conduct independent evaluations of ML/TF risk assessments
Reporting entities must evaluate policy design against Act, regulations, and AML/CTF Rules
Reporting entities must test compliance with their AML/CTF policies
Reporting entities must evaluate risk mitigation for ML/TF threats
Reporting entities must produce written reports of independent evaluations
Reporting entities must deliver independent evaluation reports to governing bodies
Reporting entities must respond to independent evaluation reports
Reporting entities must ensure reported information is complete, accurate, and unaltered
Reporting entities must establish safeguards to prevent tipping off

sections.source_quote: The AML/CTF policies of the reporting entity must deal with both initial training upon a person's employment or engagement and ongoing training during a person's employment or engagement.

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Virtual asset transfers: AML/CTF due diligence requirements
sections.summary: Reporting entities must implement specific due diligence procedures for virtual asset transfers. They must determine whether wallets are custodial or self-hosted, verify wallet controllers, assess capability to securely pass information, and mitigate money laundering risks. These obligations apply to reporting entities providing designated virtual asset transfer services.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates detailed due diligence for virtual asset transfers, requiring verification of wallet types, controller identity, and risk mitigation measures. It directly impacts cross-border data transfers involving virtual assets by imposing strict AML/CTF checks.
sections.authority: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)

requirements.text: Reporting entity must determine if wallet is custodial or self-hosted
Reporting entity must verify wallet controller identity if custodial wallet
Reporting entity must assess capability to securely pass information
Reporting entity must mitigate ML/TF risk for unverified wallet controllers

sections.source_quote: Section 5-18(3)(a) requires reporting entities to determine whether wallet is custodial or self-hosted for virtual asset transfers.

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Customer identity verification for financial services
sections.summary: Reporting entities must collect specific KYC information for customers based on their legal structure. For sole traders, they must collect business names, alternative names, unique identifiers, and business addresses. For corporates, they must collect additional details including ownership structures and beneficial owners. These requirements apply to entities providing designated financial services.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes detailed customer identity verification requirements that impact data transfer assessments by mandating comprehensive KYC information collection for different customer types, particularly affecting cross-border financial transactions.
sections.authority: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)

requirements.text: Reporting entity must collect business names for sole traders
Reporting entity must collect alternative names for customers
Reporting entity must collect unique identifiers for customers
Reporting entity must collect business addresses for customers
Reporting entity must collect ownership structures for corporates

sections.source_quote: Section 6-1(2) requires reporting entities to collect business names, alternative names, unique identifiers, and business addresses for sole traders.

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Customer identity verification for financial services
sections.summary: Reporting entities must verify customer identity and ML/TF risk when providing financial services. For individuals, they must confirm the customer's identity through reasonable steps. They must identify ML/TF risk based on KYC information and collect relevant KYC data. This applies to designated services where enhanced due diligence is not required. The reporting entity must also ensure they have AML/CTF policies to mitigate risks.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires verification of customer identity and ML/TF risk before service commencement. Mandates collection of KYC information appropriate to risk level. Affects data transfer assessments for financial services involving cross-border transactions.

requirements.text: Reporting entity must take reasonable steps to establish customer identity for individuals
Reporting entity must identify ML/TF risk based on KYC information
Reporting entity must collect KYC information appropriate to ML/TF risk
Reporting entity must establish on reasonable grounds matters in paragraphs 28(2)(a) and (c) of the Act
Reporting entity must have AML/CTF policies to mitigate additional ML/TF risk

sections.source_quote: Section 6-9: The reporting entity has taken reasonable steps to establish that the customer is the person the customer claims to be; and the reporting entity has identified the ML/TF risk of the customer, based on KYC information about the customer that is reasonably available to the reporting entity.

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Simplified customer due diligence for financial services
sections.summary: Reporting entities can apply simplified customer due diligence measures if authorized by the Act. They must verify customer identity for individuals, identify ML/TF risk, collect relevant KYC information, and ensure no reasonable grounds to doubt KYC information's adequacy or veracity. This applies to specific customer types and services where simplified measures are permitted.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows reduced due diligence for certain customers but requires verification of identity and risk assessment. Impacts data transfer assessments for services where simplified measures apply across borders.

requirements.text: Reporting entity must take reasonable steps to establish customer identity for individuals
Reporting entity must identify ML/TF risk based on KYC information
Reporting entity must collect KYC information appropriate to ML/TF risk
Reporting entity must have no reasonable grounds to doubt KYC information's adequacy or veracity

sections.source_quote: Section 6-17: the reporting entity has taken reasonable steps to establish that the customer is the person the customer claims to be; and the reporting entity has identified the ML/TF risk of the customer, based on KYC information about the customer that is reasonably available to the reporting entity.

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Nested services relationships: initial and ongoing customer due diligence requirements
sections.summary: Reporting entities must conduct initial customer due diligence for nested services relationships, including ownership structures, business complexity, country of operation, AML/CFT compliance, reputation, and risk assessment. They must also review ML/TF risk and KYC information every two years or when new services are introduced to ensure ongoing compliance with anti-money laundering and counter-terrorism financing obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates specific due diligence for nested services relationships, requiring detailed customer assessments and periodic reviews to ensure robust AML/CFT compliance when transferring services across entities.

requirements.text: Reporting entity must review ML/TF risk of customer every two years if providing designated services as part of nested services relationship
Reporting entity must review and update KYC information every two years if providing designated services as part of nested services relationship
Reporting entity must review KYC information when commencing new designated services in nested services relationship
Reporting entity must assess customer ownership, control and management structures
Reporting entity must assess customer business complexity including products, services, and customer types
Reporting entity must assess customer country of operation and AML/CFT compliance in those countries
Reporting entity must assess customer's AML/CFT systems and controls
Reporting entity must assess customer reputation based on publicly available information
Reporting entity must assess customer's history of regulatory investigations or adverse actions
Reporting entity must determine if customer can provide KYC information independently
Reporting entity must determine if customer allows shell banks to receive services
Reporting entity must determine if customer ensures no services to customers with shell bank relationships

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of paragraph 30(2)(b)(iii) of the Act, the reporting entity must review, and where appropriate, update the reporting entity's identification and assessment of the ML/TF risk of a customer if: (a) the reporting entity provides designated services to the customer as part of a nested services relationship; and (b) 2 years have elapsed since the reporting entity last identified and assessed the ML/TF risk of the customer.

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Reliance on KYC information collection and verification across international arrangements
sections.summary: Reporting entities must establish agreements with foreign entities that meet specific KYC information collection and verification requirements. These agreements must ensure timely access to KYC information, verification data, and compliance with AML/CFT obligations, with regular assessments to maintain alignment with risk profiles.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines requirements for international KYC information sharing arrangements, ensuring that data transfers for compliance purposes meet AML/CFT standards without compromising data security or privacy.

requirements.text: Agreement must involve reporting entities or persons regulated by foreign countries with FATF-compliant laws
Agreement must ensure compliance with reporting entity's obligations or equivalent foreign laws
Agreement must be appropriate to the reporting entity's ML/TF risk exposure
Agreement must enable access to KYC information before service commencement
Agreement must enable access to verification data immediately upon request
Agreement must document responsibilities for record-keeping
Reporting entity must assess agreement compliance every two years or when significant changes occur

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of paragraph 37A(1)(b) of the Act, the following requirements are prescribed in relation to an agreement or arrangement of the kind referred to in paragraph 37A(1)(a) of the Act: (a) the other party to the agreement or arrangement must be a reporting entity or a person regulated by one or more laws of a foreign country that give effect to the FATF Recommendations relating to customer due diligence and record-keeping.

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Customer due diligence for life policies and sinking fund policies
sections.summary: A reporting entity establishes customer identity for life policies or sinking fund policies if it provides services covered by items 37 or 38 of table 1 in section 6 of the Act. This requires collecting full names of potential beneficiaries or descriptive information about beneficiary classes when individual identification is impossible. The requirement applies to reporting entities conducting these specific financial services.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates specific customer identification procedures for life policies and sinking fund policies, which are critical for DTIAs involving cross-border transfers of financial data related to policy beneficiaries.

requirements.text: Reporting entity must have established on reasonable grounds customer identity for life policies or sinking fund policies
Reporting entity must collect full name of any person entitled to receive policy payment
If individual identification impossible, reporting entity must collect descriptive information about beneficiary classes

sections.source_quote: For purposes of paragraph 28(6)(b) of the Act, a reporting entity is taken to have established on reasonable grounds the matter mentioned in paragraph 28(2)(b) of the Act in relation to a customer if: (a) the designated service proposed to be provided by the reporting entity to the customer is covered by item 37 or 38 of table 1 in section 6 of the Act (services relating to a life policy or sinking fund policy); and (b) the reporting entity has: (i) collected the full name of any person who may be entitled to receive a payment under the policy; or (ii) if the nature of the policy means that it is not possible to identify each such person—collected information describing each class of persons that may be entitled to a payment under the policy.

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Correspondent banking due diligence requirements
sections.summary: Financial institutions must conduct due diligence assessments for correspondent banking relationships involving vostro accounts. This includes assessing risks of money laundering, terrorism financing, and other serious crimes, evaluating the respondent's ownership structures, business complexity, operational countries, regulatory compliance, and anti-money laundering policies. The assessments must be completed before establishing new correspondent banking relationships.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes specific due diligence requirements for correspondent banking relationships, which are critical for DTIAs as they mandate detailed risk assessments for cross-border financial data transfers involving virtual asset service providers.

requirements.text: Correspondent must assess risks of money laundering, terrorism financing, and other serious crimes
Correspondent must evaluate respondent's ownership, control, and management structures
Correspondent must assess respondent's business size, complexity, and customer types
Correspondent must evaluate respondent's operational countries and regulatory compliance
Correspondent must assess respondent's anti-money laundering and counter-terrorism financing policies

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of paragraph 96(1)(a) of the Act, this section sets out requirements for the due diligence assessment to be carried out by a financial institution (the correspondent) in relation to an entry into a correspondent banking relationship with another financial institution (the respondent) that will involve a vostro account. The due diligence assessment required to be carried out is an assessment of the risks of money laundering, financing of terrorism, proliferation financing and other serious crimes associated with the entry into the correspondent banking relationship.

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Obligations for collecting, verifying and passing on payment information
sections.summary: This section specifies information that ordering institutions must collect, verify, and pass on for different types of value transfers. It details required information for merchant payments, refunds, ATM withdrawals, and self-hosted virtual asset wallets. The obligations apply to reporting entities handling payments within Australia's financial system. The section includes specific data fields for payers and payees depending on the transfer type.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines precise data requirements for payment transfers, impacting DTIA by specifying which information must be collected, verified, and shared across different payment mechanisms. It affects cross-border transfers involving virtual assets.

requirements.text: For merchant payments, collect card number of payer used in merchant payment
For refunds of merchant payments, collect card number of payer used in refunded payment
For ATM withdrawals, collect payer's full name and card number used
For transfers to self-hosted virtual asset wallets, collect payer information and payee's full name

sections.source_quote: Section 8-3: Obligations of ordering institutions—collecting, verifying and passing on information

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Exemptions for international value transfers
sections.summary: This section outlines specific circumstances where obligations for value transfers do not apply, including transfers through foreign permanent establishments, inter-financial institution transfers, SWIFT systems, and cheques. It also provides exemptions for merchant payments and refunds, and certain existing customers. These exemptions apply to reporting entities handling value transfers under the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section identifies specific transfer scenarios exempt from data collection obligations, which is critical for DTIA as it determines when cross-border transfers require additional safeguards.

requirements.text: Exemptions apply to transfers through foreign permanent establishments meeting FATF Recommendations
Exemptions apply to inter-financial institution transfers acting on their own behalf
Exemptions apply to SWIFT system transfers between supervised financial institutions
Exemptions apply to cheques drawn on ordering institutions
Exemptions apply to merchant payments and refunds

sections.source_quote: Section 8-7: Exemptions—designated services provided at or through foreign permanent establishments

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Suspicious activity reports: required information for reporting entities
sections.summary: Reporting entities must include specific information in suspicious activity reports, including reportable details about the person involved, their relationship to the matter, and the designated service. The report must contain applicable and known information about the matter, involved persons, and transactions. Reporting entities must also provide descriptions of relationships and relevant transaction details where applicable. This applies to entities obligated to report suspicious matters under the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates detailed information reporting for suspicious activity, impacting data transfer assessments by requiring comprehensive reporting of involved persons, transaction details, and relationships across borders.

requirements.text: Report must contain reportable information about the person in relation to whom the suspicious matter reporting obligation arose
Report must contain information on the person's involvement in the matter
Report must contain reportable information about another person if required by the reportable information
Report must contain reportable information about the involved person to the extent applicable and known
Report must contain a description of the relationship between the involved person and the person in relation to whom the suspicious matter reporting obligation arose
Report must contain a description of the circumstances in paragraphs 41(1)(a) to (c) of the Act that apply to the matter
Report must contain a description of the conditions in paragraphs 41(1)(d) to (j) of the Act that are satisfied in respect of the matter
Report must contain the date and time the reporting entity commenced to provide the designated service to the first person if paragraph 41(1)(a) applies
Report must contain the date and time the first person requested the reporting entity to provide the designated service if paragraph 41(1)(b) applies
Report must contain the date and time the first person inquired of the reporting entity whether the reporting entity would be willing to provide the designated service if paragraph 41(1)(c) applies
Report must contain information on the offence against a law of the Commonwealth or a State or Territory if conditions in subparagraph 41(1)(f)(iii) are satisfied
Report must contain any addresses or locations relevant to the matter
Report must contain the reporting entity's reference number relating to the provision or proposed provision of the designated service
Report must contain a description of each designated service that relates to the matter
Report must contain the full name of the person providing the account

Report must contain information about the account holder that would be required under subsection 9-3(3) if the account holder were the person in relation to whom the suspicious matter reporting obligation arose
Report must contain the kind of account (such as a savings account, loan account or credit card account)
Report must contain the unique identification details assigned to the account (such as the BSB)
Report must contain information for each signatory to whom the matter relates that would be required under subsection 9-3(3)
Report must contain the full name of the other signatories to the account or a description of each type or class of signatory if there are more than 10 signatories
Report must contain the date the account was opened
Report must contain the balance of the account on dates relevant to the matter
Report must contain a description of the transaction
Report must contain the value associated with the transaction (in Australian dollars or a foreign currency)
Report must contain the kind and amount of foreign currency involved and the exchange rate used if the transaction involved foreign currency
Report must contain a description of the products or instruments
Report must contain the value of the products or instruments (in Australian dollars or a foreign currency)
Report must contain information for each payer or transferor of the products or instruments that would be required under subsection 9-3(3)
Report must contain the full name of the issuer and the address or location where the products or instruments were issued if the issuer is not the reporting entity
Report must contain information for each payee, transferee or beneficiary of the transaction that would be required under subsection 9-3(3)
Report must contain the date and time when the transaction took place
Report must contain the value of the money, virtual assets or property that is the subject of the transfer of value
Report must contain a unique transaction reference number
Report must contain information on the kind of virtual assets, including details of the backing asset
Report must contain the full name of any person who controls, or controlled, the virtual asset
Report must contain the full name of any person in whose name the assets are, or were, held
Report must contain the number of virtual asset units
Report must contain the value of the virtual assets in Australian dollars
Report must contain the exchange rate used in determining the value
Report must contain the unique transaction reference number, including a transaction hash
Report must contain any virtual asset wallet addresses, including destination tags or memo details
Report must contain the full name of the person who provided or proposed to provide a designated service
Report must contain the address or location where the person provided or proposed to provide a designated service
Report must contain the kind of designated service provided or proposed to be provided by the person
Report must contain unique network identification numbers for the networks used by the person
Report must contain the date and time that online activity was recorded occurring
Report must contain unique identification numbers for the relevant devices involved
Report must contain a description of the software or application being used by the person
Report must contain the full name, and any user name, used by the person for the purposes of the software or application

sections.source_quote: Section 9-4(3) The report must contain the following information, to the extent it is applicable and known to the reporting entity: (a) reportable information about the person in relation to whom the suspicious matter reporting obligation arose for the reporting entity; (b) information on the person's involvement in the matter.

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Threshold transaction reporting requirements
sections.summary: Reporting entities must include specific information in threshold transaction reports, including entity details, customer information, transaction details, and related persons. The reports must contain reportable information about individuals and non-individuals, with specific requirements for accounts, products, and property transfers. This applies to entities reporting threshold transactions under the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies detailed reporting requirements for threshold transactions, impacting data transfer assessments by mandating comprehensive information on transactions, customers, and related entities.

requirements.text: Report must contain the reporting entity's full name and the identifier assigned to the entity by AUSTRAC
Report must contain the date the report is given to the AUSTRAC CEO
Report must contain the individual's position with the reporting entity
Report must contain reportable information about the customer to the extent applicable and known
Report must contain reportable information about the transferor or transferee in the transaction (if different to the customer)
Report must contain reportable information about another person if the customer received the designated service on behalf of another person
Report must contain a description of the relationship between the person and the customer
Report must contain a description of the authority for the person to act on behalf of the customer
Report must contain reportable information about the person

Report must contain a statement as to the circumstances of the designated service if the customer was represented by a person in circumstances where the threshold transaction was a deposit with no personal contact
Report must contain a statement as to the circumstances of the designated service if the transaction did not involve a virtual asset
Report must contain a statement as to the circumstances of the designated service if the person was acting in the course of a business of collecting, holding or delivering physical currency
Report must contain the date and time when the transaction took place
Report must contain the address or location where the transaction took place
Report must contain the person's role in the transaction (to the extent known)
Report must contain a description of the purpose of the transaction (to the extent known)
Report must contain the kind of designated service that was provided and that involved the transaction
Report must contain the reporting entity's reference number relating to the provision of the designated service
Report must contain the value, in Australian dollars, of the physical currency involved in the transaction
Report must contain the kind and amount of foreign currency involved and the exchange rate used if the transaction involves foreign currency
Report must contain the full name of the person providing the account
Report must contain the full name of each account holder
Report must contain the kind of account (such as a savings account, loan account or credit card account)
Report must contain the unique identification details assigned to the account (such as the BSB)
Report must contain the full name of the signatory to the account who is conducting the transaction
Report must contain the date the account was opened
Report must contain a description of the products or instruments and their value

sections.source_quote: Section 9-8(1) The report must contain the following information about the threshold transaction: (a) the date and time when the transaction took place; (b) the address or location where the transaction took place; (c) for each person in relation to whom information is reportable under section 9-7—the person's role in the transaction (to the extent known); (d) a description of the purpose of the transaction (to the extent known); (e) the kind of designated service that was provided and that involved the transaction; (f) the reporting entity's reference number relating to the provision of the designated service that involved the transaction (including, but not limited to, a transaction reference number); (g) the value, in Australian dollars, of the physical currency involved in the transaction; (h) if the transaction involves foreign currency—the kind and amount of the foreign currency involved and the exchange rate used.

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Reporting requirements for virtual assets and cross-border monetary instruments
sections.summary: This section mandates detailed reporting for virtual assets and monetary instruments moving across borders. Reporting entities must disclose specific information about virtual assets, including their type, value, and controlling persons, when transactions exceed thresholds. It also requires comprehensive details about individuals or entities moving monetary instruments into or out of Australia, including personal identifiers, addresses, and financial details. These obligations apply to reporting entities under the Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC) framework.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes strict data reporting requirements for cross-border monetary instrument transfers and virtual assets, necessitating detailed personal and financial information. It affects DTIAs by requiring thorough data collection and validation of cross-border transactions to prevent illicit financial flows.

requirements.text: Reports must include information on virtual assets, including their type, backing asset, controlling persons, and value in Australian dollars
Reports must specify the full name of persons controlling virtual assets, including decentralised autonomous organisations
Reports must include the number of virtual asset units and exchange rate used to determine value
Reports must contain unique transaction reference numbers and virtual asset wallet addresses
Reports must include details of individuals moving monetary instruments into or out of Australia
Reports must contain full name, date of birth, citizenship, residential address, and telephone number for individuals
Reports must include ABN, ACN, and ARBN details for non-individual entities
Reports must specify the monetary instrument type, amount, and currency being moved
Reports must include details of the person receiving monetary instruments
Reports must be submitted within specific timeframes depending on movement method

sections.source_quote: Section 9-8(5) states: 'If the threshold transaction relates to virtual assets, the report must contain the following information about the virtual assets (as applicable and to the extent that the information is known): (a) information on the kind of virtual assets, including details of the backing asset (if any); (b) the full name of any person who controls, or controlled, the virtual asset (including a decentralised autonomous organisation, if applicable); ...'

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Cross-border monetary instrument reporting obligations
sections.summary: This section establishes reporting requirements for monetary instruments moving across Australia's borders. It specifies detailed information that must be included in reports for individuals or entities moving monetary instruments into or out of Australia, including personal identifiers, addresses, and financial details. The reporting must be completed within specific timeframes depending on the movement method, with penalties for non-compliance. These obligations apply to reporting entities under AUSTRAC's framework.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires comprehensive data collection for cross-border monetary transfers, impacting DTIAs by mandating detailed personal and financial information validation to ensure compliance with Australian financial regulations.

requirements.text: Reports must include full name, date of birth, citizenship, residential address, and telephone number for individuals moving monetary instruments
Reports must contain ABN, ACN, and ARBN details for non-individual entities
Reports must specify the monetary instrument type, amount, and currency being moved
Reports must include details of the person receiving monetary instruments
Reports must be submitted within specific timeframes depending on movement method

sections.source_quote: Section 9-12(2) states: 'For the purposes of paragraph (1)(a), the information is as follows: (a) if the person is an individual: (i) the person's full name; (ii) the person's date and place of birth; ... (ix) the person's ABN (if any);'

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Transitional reporting requirements for suspicious and threshold transactions
sections.summary: This section establishes transitional requirements for reporting suspicious matters and threshold transactions in the first three months after the instrument's commencement. Reporting entities must use the approved form from before the instrument's commencement and comply with specific Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Rules. Reports must be submitted within defined timeframes and include specific information to satisfy the reporting obligations under the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires specific reporting formats and timelines for suspicious and threshold transactions during transitional periods, impacting data transfer and reporting practices across borders for financial institutions.

requirements.text: Reports must be in the form approved immediately before commencement
Reports must comply with Chapter 18 of the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Rules Instrument 2007
Reports must be submitted within 3 months after commencement
Reports must include information required under Division 1 of Part 9

sections.source_quote: 12-1  Transitional—reports of suspicious matters to be in old form for first 3 months after commencement (1) Subsections (2) and (3) apply if: (a) a suspicious matter reporting obligation arises for a reporting entity in relation to a person; and (b) the reporting entity is required under subsection 41(2) of the Act to give a report about the matter to the AUSTRAC CEO at any time in the period: (i) beginning at the commencement of this instrument; and (ii) ending on 30 June 2026.

sections.heading: Section 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Extension notices for keep open notices
sections.summary: This section details the requirements for extension notices that can prolong the validity of keep open notices. The extension notices must be issued by a senior member of an agency, specify a further 6-month period, and maintain exemptions from certain sections of the Act. The notices do not compel reporting entities to continue services but may trigger suspicious matter reporting obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows for extended periods of exemptions from certain financial regulations through keep open notices, impacting data transfer and processing practices across borders for financial institutions.

requirements.text: Extension notices extend keep open notices by a further 6 months
Extension notices must be issued to the reporting entity before the expiry of the keep open notice
Senior members must reasonably believe continued service provision assists investigation
Reporting entities remain exempt from sections 26G, 28 or 30 of the Act

sections.source_quote: Form 2—Extension notice: This extension notice extends the period that a keep open notice remains in force for a further period of 6 months.
